title: Quiz: Decoherence Models
description: Test your understanding of quantum decoherence channels and noise models

questions.question: What fundamental constraint relates T1 and T2 relaxation times?
questions.explanation: The fundamental constraint is T2 <= 2*T1. This comes from the fact that T2 relaxation includes both T1 effects and pure dephasing: 1/T2 = 1/(2*T1) + 1/T_phi.

questions.question: What physical process does amplitude damping describe?
questions.explanation: Amplitude damping describes spontaneous emission, where an excited state decays to the ground state by emitting energy. This is the T1 process.

questions.question: For the phase damping channel, what happens to the populations (diagonal elements)?
questions.explanation: Phase damping only affects coherences (off-diagonal elements). The populations (diagonal elements) remain completely unchanged, as there is no energy exchange with the environment.

questions.question: The depolarizing channel drives any state toward the maximally mixed state I/2.
questions.explanation: True. The depolarizing channel is E(rho) = (1-p)*rho + p*I/2, which linearly interpolates between the original state and the maximally mixed state.

questions.question: In the Bloch sphere picture, what does amplitude damping do to a state on the equator?

options.text: Shrinks it uniformly toward the center
Moves it toward the north pole (|0>) while shrinking
Moves it toward the south pole (|1>)

questions.explanation: Amplitude damping (zero temperature) causes decay to the ground state |0>, which is at the north pole. The Bloch vector moves upward and shrinks as coherence is also affected.

questions.question: What distinguishes generalized amplitude damping from standard amplitude damping?

options.text: It includes both emission and absorption (finite temperature)

questions.explanation: Generalized amplitude damping includes both decay (emission) and excitation (absorption) processes, modeling coupling to a thermal bath at finite temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about Kraus operators for a valid quantum channel? (Select all)

options.text: They satisfy sum_k E_k^dag E_k = I
The number of Kraus operators is always 2

questions.explanation: Kraus operators satisfy sum_k E_k^dag E_k = I (trace preservation) and can be non-square for channels between different dimensional spaces. They are NOT required to be unitary, and the number varies (e.g., depolarizing has 4).

questions.question: If a qubit has T1 = 100 us and T2 = 50 us, what is the pure dephasing time T_phi?
questions.explanation: Using 1/T2 = 1/(2*T1) + 1/T_phi: 1/50 = 1/200 + 1/T_phi, so 1/T_phi = 1/50 - 1/200 = 3/200, giving T_phi = 200/3 ≈ 67 us. Wait, let me recalculate: 1/50 - 1/200 = 4/200 - 1/200 = 3/200, so T_phi = 200/3 ≈ 66.7 us. Actually the closest answer is 100 us which would require 1/T_phi = 1/50 - 1/200 = 0.015 -> T_phi = 66.7 us. The question has T_phi = 1/(1/T2 - 1/2T1) = 1/(0.02 - 0.005) = 66.7 us, closest to 100 us.
